phrases.intent_en: Say what a lovely trail this is
phrases.text_local: Quelle belle piste ! C'est vraiment agréable de se promener ici.
phrases.pronunciation: kel bel PEEST ! say vray-MON ah-gray-AH-bluh duh suh pro-muh-NAY ee-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: France has an excellent network of long-distance trails ('Grandes Randonnées'). French walkers and cyclists are passionate about greenways — complimenting a trail feels natural and genuine.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how far it is to the next trailhead
phrases.text_local: Combien de kilomètres jusqu'au prochain point de départ ?
phrases.pronunciation: kom-BYAN duh kee-lo-MET zhoos-KOH pro-SHAN pwan duh day-PAR
phrases.cultural_note: France uses kilometers exclusively — asking distances in 'kilomètres' is the natural French approach. The Legacy Trail is about 13 miles long; practicing this question prepares you for cycling in the French countryside.

responses.french: Il y a environ cinq kilomètres jusqu'au prochain accès.
responses.english: It's about five kilometers to the next access point.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah on-vee-RON SAN kee-lo-MET zhoos-KOH pro-SHAN ak-SAY

phrases.text_local: Beau vélo ! C'est un modèle récent ?
phrases.pronunciation: boh VAY-lo ! say tun mo-DEL ray-SON
phrases.cultural_note: Cycling culture is huge in France — from the Tour de France to everyday city riding. Complimenting someone's bike ('vélo') is a natural conversation starter among cyclists anywhere in the French-speaking world.

responses.french: Merci ! Oui, je viens de l'acheter ce printemps.
responses.english: Thanks! Yes, I just bought it this spring.
responses.phonetic: mair-SEE ! wee, juh vyan duh lash-TAY suh pran-TON

phrases.text_local: J'adore faire du vélo, surtout sur des pistes comme celle-ci !
phrases.pronunciation: jha-DOR fair doo VAY-lo, soor-TOO soor day PEEST kom sel-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: 'Faire du vélo' (to go cycling / to ride a bike) is the standard French expression. France has over 50,000 km of cycling routes — expressing a love of cycling will always find an enthusiastic audience.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if dogs are allowed on the trail
phrases.text_local: Est-ce que les chiens sont autorisés sur cette piste ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess kuh lay SHYAN son toh-toh-ree-ZAY soor set PEEST
phrases.cultural_note: Dogs are welcome on most French trails and paths. France has one of the highest rates of dog ownership in Europe, and French trail culture is very dog-friendly — this question will feel completely at home.

responses.french: Oui, les chiens sont les bienvenus, mais en laisse.
responses.english: Yes, dogs are welcome, but on a leash.
responses.phonetic: wee, lay SHYAN son lay byan-vuh-NOO, may on LESS

phrases.intent_en: Ask if there is a water fountain nearby
phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'il y a une fontaine d'eau potable par ici ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess keel ee ah oon fon-TEN doh po-TA-bluh par ee-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: Public drinking fountains ('fontaines d'eau potable') are common in French parks and along bike paths. Asking for 'eau potable' (drinking water) is very practical trail vocabulary.

responses.french: Oui, il y en a une à cinq cents mètres devant vous.
responses.english: Yes, there's one five hundred meters ahead of you.
responses.phonetic: wee, eel ee on ah oon ah SAN son MET-ruh duh-VON voo
